paragraphs.summary: This statement is relevant to CRR Firms.1

It sets out the Prudential Regulation Authority’s (PRA’s) expectations on the quality of regulatory capital resources that firms are required to hold under the CRR. This statement complements the requ

As set out in ‘The PRA’s approach to banking supervision’, the PRA expects the most significant part of a firm’s capital to be ordinary shares and reserves. These are the highest-quality form of capit

When assessing firms, the PRA will be mindful of the fact that quality of capital is not purely about whether a firm meets each sub-tier of the capital rules. For example, even if two firms have ident

As set out in ‘The PRA’s approach to banking supervision’, the PRA also expects firms to comply with the clearly stated internationally agreed criteria around the definition of capital, in spirit as w
With that purpose in mind, the PRA’s preference is for firms to adopt simple, plain vanilla CET1 share structures consisting of only one class of share that is fully subordinated to all other capital

The PRA expects firms to refrain from features that may be ineffective (or less effective) in absorbing losses. For the avoidance of doubt, this expectation also applies to Additional Tier 1 (AT1) and

Complexity can arise, for instance, when CET1 shareholders have different rights and entitlements, including preferential realisation provisions or other features that guarantee a distribution to CET1

The PRA expects the relevant Senior Management Function (SMF) to take responsibility for ensuring the quality of the capital structure overall. This includes being accountable for the quality of notif

The PRA expects the SMF’s proposal, in turn, to be subject to appropriate board-level review and discussion and the board should consider and suggest ways to minimise any proposed complexity. In cases

Own Funds (CRR) Articles 52 and 54 require AT1 instruments to contain a trigger of at least 5.125% CET1, but allows firms to select a higher trigger. They also recognise that the terms of an AT1 instr
Depending on the circumstances, an instrument with a trigger of 5.125% CET1 may not convert in time to prevent the failure of a firm. A temporary write-down may make it more difficult for the firm to
Own Funds (CRR) Article 52 is indifferent to the equity or liability accounting classification of an AT1 instrument. Most of the AT1 instru
Firms will wish to consider these factors when deciding how to exercise the choices available to them under the Own Funds (CRR) Part. The PRA expects to discuss with firms

Where possible, the PRA expects firms to meet their CET1 requirements entirely with voting common shares and associated reserves. The PRA strongly discourages firms from including non-voting shares in

sections.chapter_title: Subordination, remedies, events of default and set-off

paragraphs.summary: Under the Own Funds (CRR) Part, all regulatory capital must be capable of absorbing losses either on a going or gone concern basis. Therefore, all capital instruments as a

It is also important that subordination is not made less effective by granting additional rights to holders of subordinated instruments, for example in respect of events of default, remedies and right

In the event that default occurs, the PRA expects remedies to be restricted, to the fullest extent permitted under the laws of the relevant jurisdictions, to petitioning for the winding-up of the firm

Also, to the fullest extent permitted under the laws of the relevant jurisdictions, the PRA expects subordinated creditors to waive any rights to set off amounts they owe the issuer against subordinat
As announced in the PRA statement on 29 June 2013 and reiterated in PS7/13,

For the purposes of valuation, the PRA considers that the embedded value method is not appropriate for determining the value of firms’ significant insurance holdings. This is because the embedded valu

sections.chapter_title: Connected funding of a capital nature (CFCN)

paragraphs.summary: Definition of Capital Part Chapter 4 states that firms must treat all CFCN as a holding of capital of the connected party and apply
Firms should take account of contractual, structural, reputational or other factors when determining whether a transaction is a CFCN.

Lending to a connected party will not normally be considered CFCN where that party is acting as a vehicle to pass funding to an unconnected party and has no other creditors whose claims could be senio
Additionally, for connected parties within the same consolidation group, it is likely that a loan is not CFCN if:

Firms are generally required6 to notify the PRA at least one month before the intended date of issuance or amendment or variation to the terms of each CET1 or AT1 capital instrument, and immediately a

The PRA is likely to need more time to review a notified instrument with complex feature(s) (as set out in paragraphs [2.3](/guidance/supervisory-statements/ss07-13-definition-of-capital---crr-firms#8
The PRA expects the relevant SMF (as defined in paragraph 2.5 above) to ensure that the n
In certain cases, as set out in Definition of Capital Part Rule 7A – [7D](/pra-rules/definition-of-capital#85d6ebbe18d046efa77ca380

The PRA may ask firms to provide additional information, for example in case of an incomplete notification, unclear terms and conditions or changes to terms and conditions during the assessment period
CRR II allows a firm to count any subsequent issuance of a form of CET1 instrument for which it has already received the PRA’s permission (pursuant to CRR Article 26(3) (as amended)) towards its CET1

The required timing of a PIN submission depends on the characteristics of the notified instrument. The following table summarises the notification requirements set out in [Definition of Capital Part R
CET1 issuances whose terms and conditions (including any side agreements) are identical to those of an issuance for which a firm has already received the PRA’s permission would satisfy the conditions
Firms should exercise reasonable judgment when determining whether an instrument is ‘substantially the same’ as one previously reviewed by the PRA, and contact their supervisor in case of ambiguity.
A CET1 instrument will normally not be considered substantially the same as one previously reviewed by the PRA if:
In any such cases, firms should notify the PRA at least one month in advance of the intended date of issuance.

The PRA considers an AT1 instrument to be substantially the same as one previously reviewed by the PRA if its terms and conditions (including any side agreements) are identical to a previous AT1 instr
For subsequent issuances of AT1 instruments on such terms, firms may notify the PRA no later than the intended date of the subsequent issuance.
An AT1 issuance will normally not be considered substantially the same as one previously reviewed by the PRA if:
The PRA considers a Tier 2 instrument to be substantially the same if its terms and conditions (including any side agreements) are identical to a previous Tier 2 instrument except for the issue date,

sections.chapter_title: Timing of inclusion of interim profits in CET1

paragraphs.summary: Where interim profits have been verified for a particular period pursuant to Own Funds (CRR) Article 26(2), they may be included in capital

For example: capital adequacy reporting for the period ended 30 June is due on 11 August. A firm receives verification of its interim profits for the period ended 30 June between that date and 11 Augu
